experiences.intentions:
Player 1: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Servant on round 1, an initial 2-person team is often acceptable unless there is a clear reason to distrust it. Approving preserves flexibility and may generate information from the quest result and later reactions. My vote itself reveals little beyond willingness to test an uninformative opening team, which is generally fine for Good.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so team [0,2] is fully controlled by evil. On team vote in round 1, approving an all-evil 2-player team is optimal because it gives us a chance to fail quest 1 immediately. My approval is consistent with wanting this mission to go through, and while it may slightly associate me with 2, evil benefits more from securing the mission than from distancing here.
Player 3: I know players 0 and 2 are Evil from Merlin information, so the proposed team [0,2] is entirely evil. In team voting as Good, the optimal play is to reject any team containing known evil players, especially quest 1 where preventing an evil-only team is critical. My reject vote is consistent with protecting Good, and while it may slightly signal suspicion, allowing this team through would be far worse.
Player 4: No concrete role information yet from proposals or votes. As Good Servant on round 1 quest 1, a 2-person team including the proposer is fairly standard and early approval gives information from quest result without risking too much. Rejecting without evidence slows the game and may help Evil create noise. My vote reveals I am willing to test an uncontroversial opening team, which is generally good for Good.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team is exactly both evil players in a 5-player game. As evil, the optimal play is to get evil-heavy teams approved early and secure a fail on quest 1. Voting to approve is strongly aligned with my side's goal. My approve vote is not especially revealing because leaders often support their own team and evil may want this through; while some may later suspect the team composition, rejecting would only hurt evil's chances here.

My pass reveals little individually and helps our team avoid obvious exposure.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 1, 3, and 4 are unknown, though player 3 rejected the 0/2 team which may indicate caution or good alignment. This is quest 1 on a 2-player team, and as evil on the team, the optimal play is usually to pass to avoid immediately exposing both evil players and to preserve cover for later quests. A fail here would strongly implicate 0 and 2 since both are evil and on the team, which hurts our team more than the early point helps.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I know players 0 and 2 are Evil, so team [1,3,4] contains no known Evil and includes me as Merlin. In team voting, Good should approve clean teams that avoid known Evil. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a strong team, and rejecting would only hinder Good.
Player 4: Quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 are slightly more likely good, though a single success doesn't clear anyone. Proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both prior quest members and includes me plus two unknowns, which gives little continuity and higher risk. As a good Servant in team voting, I should favor teams with at least some positive evidence. Rejecting this team pressures leaders toward including at least one of 0 or 2 and reveals a consistency preference that helps good more than evil.
Player 2: I am Evil Minion with teammate 0. Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,2], which can happen because we intentionally passed to avoid outing both evil players early. Now proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both known evil players, so it is very likely all-good and would likely succeed, putting Good to 2-0. As evil, optimal play is to reject good-only teams and try to get at least one evil onto future quests. My vote reveals some opposition to a clean team, but a reject is standard for evil here and helps prevent a dangerous success.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,2], which means we intentionally hid evil by passing; that gives us some cover. Current phase is team voting on [1,3,4], a team with no known evil on it, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and put Good up 2-0. As evil, optimal play is to reject good-only teams, especially on 5-player where quest 2 has size 3 and success gives strong town momentum. My reject is consistent with wanting more information / skepticism after an all-new trio, and does not reveal too much since mixed voting is normal.
Player 1: I only know quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, which weakly suggests 0 and 2 are more likely good but does not clear them because evil can pass early. I have no direct evidence on 3 or 4. As a Servant, I want to approve teams that are reasonably likely to succeed while gathering information. This proposed 3-person team includes me, which guarantees one good member, and on a 5-player game quest 2 requires only one fail to fail, so uncertainty on both 3 and 4 is risky; however rejecting round-1 teams without stronger evidence is often unnecessarily conservative. Approving reveals little beyond willingness to test a new combination, and a success/failure here gives useful info on 3 and 4 relative to me.

My action reveals only that at least one of {0,2} is likely evil, but ambiguity remains due to player 4 also being present to some observers; overall this helps Evil more than it hurts.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes known Evil 0, so as Good I should reject. Rejecting also avoids sending a known evil on quest and signals opposition to suspicious teams without exposing too much beyond normal good play.
Player 1: I’m Good with no private info. Quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and/or 2 are somewhat better than random. Quest 2 team [0,2,4] got 2 fails, which strongly implies 2 and 4 are Evil and 0 is likely Good. Current proposed team [0,1] excludes the two strongest Evil candidates and includes me, a known Good. On team voting as Good, approving a highly likely clean 2-player team is optimal. My approval also signals support for the deduction that 2 and 4 are suspicious.
Player 4: I’m Good and not on this team. Team [0,2] previously succeeded, while my team [0,2,4] failed with 2 fails, which strongly implies both evil players are among {0,2,4}. Since [0,2] had 0 fails, 4 is almost certainly good and exactly one of 0 or 2 is evil; plus the other evil is also among {0,2,4}, so the pair must be 0 and 2. That makes player 1 likely good. Proposed team [0,1] contains suspected evil 0, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending likely evil on quest. Rejecting also signals my strong read that 0 and 2 are the evil pair.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,2,4] got 2 fails, confirming both 0 and I were on it; player 4 is likely good from that result. Proposed team [0,1] contains one known evil (0) and one unknown/good-leaning player. As evil in team voting, I generally want to approve teams containing evil, especially a 2-player team with exactly one evil, since 0 can fail and secure another evil point while creating uncertainty about player 1. Approving also aligns with not signaling anti-0 behavior.
Player 0: I am evil Assassin with teammate 2. Quest 1 on [0,2] succeeded despite both evil being on it, likely to build cover; quest 2 on [0,2,4] failed with 2 fails, strongly clearing 4 as likely good and making 0/2 look suspicious. Current proposed team [0,1] includes me but excludes teammate 2. On a 2-person quest, I can fail if approved and secure evil lead, but the team is somewhat suspicious because it includes me after the double-fail team. As evil, optimal is usually to approve any team containing me, since it gives direct sabotage opportunity. Voting approve is consistent with wanting this team through; rejecting my own team would be odd and may reveal me further.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 [0,1] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/1 is evil. Quest 2 [0,2,4] had 2 fails, and since I am good, both 0 and 2 must be evil. Therefore 1 and 3 are good by elimination. Proposed team [1,3,4] is all good and should be approved. Approving reveals confidence in the solved table and helps Good secure quest 4, while rejecting risks unnecessary delay.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,2,4] got 2 fails, confirming both evil were on it and making 4 look somewhat good from others' perspective. Current proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both known evil players, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, tying the game 2-2 and reducing evil control. As evil on a team vote, optimal play is to reject clean good teams, especially on quest 4 where a success is dangerous. My reject also aligns with preserving ambiguity; approving a team without any suspected evil could help good too much.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,2,4] got 2 fails, confirming 4 likely good or at least not needed for evil coordination; Quest 3 team [0,1] got 1 fail, so 1 is likely good while 0 or I supplied the fail previously. Current proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both evils 0 and 2, so it is very likely all-good and would succeed, giving good their second point and narrowing the game. As evil on team voting, optimal play is to reject any team without an evil player, especially at quest 4 with evil already at 2 wins. My reject signals little because rejecting all-good teams is standard for evil, and good players may also reject from uncertainty.
Player 3: I know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both known evil players and matches the only successful pattern we’ve seen involving me with 1 and 4; on quest 4, good must stop evil from reaching 3 wins, so approving a team with no known evil is optimal. My vote supports a clean team and doesn’t reveal more than necessary beyond preferring teams without 0/2, which is consistent with good play.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 succeeded with unknown team, Quest 2 team [0,2,4] had 2 fails so at least two of {0,2,4} are Evil, making 1 and 3 relatively better. Quest 3 team [0,1] had 1 fail; since I know I am Good, player 0 must be Evil. Therefore from [0,2,4], with 0 Evil and 2 fails there, at least one of 2 or 4 is also Evil. Proposed team [1,3,4] includes me and likely-good 3, but also 4 who is suspicious. On a 3-person Quest 4, one fail is enough to lose, and Good is already down 1-2, so we should reject risky teams. Rejecting reveals I am consistent with the deduction that 4 is suspicious and helps Good seek a safer team like [1,2,3] or [1,3 plus whoever is less bad], though likely 2/4 are both bad? In any case this team is not trustworthy.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,4] contains both known evil players, so as Good the optimal play is to reject. Approving would almost guarantee another failed quest and gives no beneficial info to Good.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate and I am evil. Team [0,2,4] contains both evil players, so if approved we can guarantee a fail and likely win immediately on quest 4. As evil, the optimal play is to approve a team with both evils on it. My vote may look somewhat suspicious, but securing the quest failure is worth it and consistent with prior team composition that already failed.
Player 0: I know player 2 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,2,4] contains both evils, which is excellent for securing another fail on quest 4 and likely ending the game before assassination matters. Since evil already leads 2-1, the optimal play is to approve any team containing both evils. My approval is consistent with wanting this team through; even if noticed later, evil benefits more from the immediate chance to fail the quest than from subtlety now.
Player 4: I’m Good with no special info. From results: quest 1 [0,1] had 1 fail so at least one of 0/1 is evil. Quest 2 [0,2,4] had 2 fails, and since I’m Good, both 0 and 2 must be evil. That fits quest 3 [1,3,4] being rejected by many; likely good players objected. Current proposed team repeats two highly suspicious players 0 and 2 on a must-win quest for Good, so optimal play is to reject. Rejecting reveals I’m consistent with anti-evil reads and avoids handing Evil the game.
Player 1: Two failed quests heavily implicate team [0,2,4]: quest 3 with exactly those three had 2 fails, so at least two of {0,2,4} are evil. Quest 2 [0,1] had 1 fail, and since I am good, player 0 is evil. Therefore among {2,4}, at least one is also evil, and in fact quest 3 suggests both 2 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [0,2,4] is therefore essentially all evil and should be rejected. As good in team voting, rejecting prevents an evil-controlled quest from passing/failing again and reveals consistency with anti-evil reasoning.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: We are Good Servant at 1-2 with quest 4 critical. Team [1,3,4] previously produced a fail on quest 2, so at least one of 1 or 3 is evil (since I know 4/me is good). This exact team is therefore guaranteed bad. Optimal good play is reject. My reject signals consistency that this lineup contains a known suspect and avoids handing evil a likely winning mission.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil, so 1 and 4 are the only likely good players besides me. The proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both known evils and is the strongest possible 3-player team. At 1-2 on quest 4, approving the all-good team is optimal. My vote reveals little because this is the obvious good team to support.
Player 0: I am evil Assassin with teammate 2. Team [1,3,4] contains no known evil, so if approved it likely succeeds, giving Good a second point and making score 2-2 before final quest. As evil, I should prefer rejecting all-good teams and pushing for a team containing 0 or 2. The prior voting suggests 1/3/4 are plausibly good since this same trio was rejected before; approving would reveal I may support a clean team. Rejecting aligns with evil interests and keeps pressure for a sabotage-capable team later.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Previous fails came from [0,1] with 1 fail and an earlier approved team with 2 fails, strongly suggesting at least one of 1/3/4 is evil too, but not certain which. Current phase is team voting on quest 4, and evil already leads 2-1, so the optimal play is to approve any team that plausibly contains an evil player and reject teams that look too clean. This team [1,3,4] excludes both known evils 0 and 2, but given the 2-fail quest result from an approved team before, it is very likely one of 1/3/4 is evil, making this a strong chance to win immediately. Approving also avoids signaling too clearly that I know the team composition from evil info.
Player 1: We are Good Servant. Two failed quests mean both evil are likely among players 0,2,4 because quest 1 [0,1] had 1 fail so 0 is likely evil (I am good), and quest 2 [1,3,4] had 2 fails total in history? Actually recent log shows my proposed [1,3,4] was approved and quest failed with 2 fail votes, which implies both 3 and 4 are evil since I am good. But with 5 players only 2 evil, so that reading makes 3 and 4 evil and also 0 evil, impossible. More likely logs are ordered newest-first: approved [1,3,4] and quest failed with 1 fail? Still uncertain. Current proposed team [1,3,4] includes me plus 3 and 4, and this exact team was previously rejected by majority, suggesting suspicion. Given Good is down 1-2, approving a risky team is bad. Optimal good play is reject teams containing unresolved suspicious pairs when not forced by 5th rejection. My vote reveals caution, which helps good coordinate.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: We are evil Assassin with teammate 2. Score is Good 1 - Evil 2, so one more failed quest wins immediately for evil before assassination matters. On a 5-player game, quest 4 team size is 3 and only one fail is needed. Team is [0,2,4], containing both evil players 0 and 2. Since a fail ends the game for evil, optimal play is to sabotage now. Information revealed doesn't matter because winning outright is best.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; team [0,2,4] has two evils, so either of us can sabotage. We already lead 2-1 in quests, and winning quest 4 ends the game immediately for evil, so optimal play is to ensure this quest fails. A fail reveals evil presence on the team, but since this is the fourth quest and would secure the third failed quest for evil, information leakage no longer matters compared to guaranteeing victory.
Player 4: I am a Good Servant on the quest team, so I must vote pass. Prior results and voting suggest suspicion around players 1 and 3 since [1,3,4] was repeatedly proposed/rejected and two fails already occurred elsewhere, but as a Servant on-quest my optimal play is always to support the mission. Voting pass reveals nothing harmful beyond consistent Good behavior and is required for my alignment.
